name: El Alto Tajo in gravel
description:
Large gorges, turquoise waters, towns with history and a privileged environment to enjoy nature and visit the karst geomorphology. I suggest you pedal along this spectacular Route that runs through the Alto Tajo Natural Park, in the province of Guadalajara.

The start and end point of the route is Molina de Aragón. It is a very interesting town of medieval origin, head of the region, which offers a wide variety of accommodation and restaurants. I recommend a visit to its historic center with its Jewish quarter and the huge castle that presides over it.

In total, the route is 120 kilometers long and 1750 meters of elevation gain. It can be done in a single day. I recommend that if you have time, do it in two stages so you can enjoy it without rushing: the first to Peralejos de las Truchas and the second, back to Molina de Aragón.

The route runs through large dry farming fields until reaching the town of Taravilla. From there you will ascend to the wooded hills and passes to end up descending to Peralejo de las Truchas, the conceptual halfway point of the trip, where you can rest and recharge before entering the Tajo canyons, the most impressive part of the Route.

This second part takes place mostly on good tracks, a small section of non-cyclable path and a last section on a quiet road.

Upon reaching the San Pedro bridge, you will leave the Tagus River to take and go up the Gallo River canyon, until you return to Molina de Aragón.

I recommend that you make a stop to visit the sanctuary of the Virgen de la Hoz and, if you have time, walk up to the La Hoz viewpoint: a perfect ending to the trip.

It is very cold here in winter. It is one of the coldest areas of the Iberian Peninsula, so I recommend you come in the less hot parts of the summer.
region_intro: Gravel biking around the Tajo region offers diverse terrain shaped by the Tagus River and its tributaries. The landscape features extensive pine forests, deep canyons, and unique rock formations characteristic of the Iberian Mountain Range. This area provides a network of unpaved tracks and forest roads, making it suitable for various gravel cycling experiences. The region's varied topography includes both challenging climbs and scenic riverside paths. Best gravel bike trails around the Tajo The most popular gravel bike trail is El Alto Tajo in gravel, a 73.7 miles (118.7 km) trail that takes 10 hours 7 minutes to complete. This difficult route traverses the dramatic landscapes of the Alto Tajo Natural Park. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Castle of Oreja – Royal Palace of Aranjuez loop from Aranjuez, a difficult 62.9 miles (101.4 km) path. This route explores historical sites and riverside woodlands near Aranjuez. Local gravel bikers also love the Talavera de la Reina – Calera y Chozas loop from Talavera de la Reina, a 59.7 miles (96.0 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 7 hours 45 minutes. Gravel biking around the Tajo is defined by deep canyons, extensive pine forests, and well-preserved riverbanks. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to different ability levels. The routes in Tajo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 9 reviews. More than 250 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Tajo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many gravel bike trails are available in the Tajo region?
A: The Tajo region offers a wide variety of gravel biking experiences, with over 60 routes listed on komoot. These range from easy riverside paths to challenging climbs through the canyons, catering to different skill levels.

Q: What are the general difficulty levels for gravel biking in Tajo?
A: Gravel biking in the Tajo region offers routes for all abilities. You'll find a good mix of challenges, with around 30 moderate and nearly 30 difficult routes, alongside a few easier options. The terrain is diverse, featuring both gentle riverside sections and demanding climbs through the dramatic canyons of the Alto Tajo Natural Park.

Q: Are there any family-friendly gravel biking routes in the Tajo area?
A: While many routes in Tajo are moderate to difficult, there are easier sections and paths suitable for families, particularly along flatter riverside areas. For a moderate option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23743909" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talavera de la Reina – La Portiña Reservoir loop from Talavera de la Reina&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a manageable distance and elevation.

Q: Are there any circular gravel bike routes in the Tajo region?
A: Yes, many of the gravel routes in the Tajo region are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31494743" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Oreja – Royal Palace of Aranjuez loop from Aranjuez&lt;/a&gt; offers a historical and scenic circular ride. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31499245" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talavera de la Reina – Calera y Chozas loop from Talavera de la Reina&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of natural attractions can I expect to see along the gravel trails?
A: The Tajo region, especially within the Alto Tajo Natural Park, is rich in natural beauty. You'll encounter dramatic canyons and gorges, extensive pine forests, and unique rock formations. The Tagus River and its tributaries carve through the landscape, creating beautiful riverside woodlands. Notable natural highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5876460" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Garganta del Fraile&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7249951" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Stone Waterfall (Cascada de Piedra)&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest along the routes?
A: Yes, the Tajo region blends natural beauty with historical significance. Routes near Aranjuez often pass by historical sites like the Royal Palace. You might also encounter impressive viewpoints such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/614479" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Enebros Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4041758" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoints of Las Barrancas and Castrejón Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, offering glimpses into the region's past and stunning vistas.

Q: What is the best time of year for gravel biking in Tajo?
A: The Tajo region is generally enjoyable for gravel biking during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers can be hot, especially in the canyons, so early mornings or late afternoons are preferable if riding during this season. Spring brings lush greenery, while autumn offers beautiful foliage.

Q: Are there any long-distance gravel biking opportunities in the Tajo region?
A: Absolutely. The Tajo region is well-suited for long-distance gravel biking and bikepacking, particularly within the Alto Tajo Natural Park. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e825038957" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Alto Tajo in gravel&lt;/a&gt;, at nearly 119 km, traverse significant distances through wild, depopulated areas, often referred to as 'Spanish Lapland,' using extensive forest tracks.

Q: What do other gravel bikers say about the Tajo region?
A: The Tajo region is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 9 reviews. Riders often praise the diverse terrain, the stunning natural landscapes of the Alto Tajo Natural Park, and the extensive network of unpaved tracks that make for excellent gravel cycling adventures.

Q: Can I find routes that start near Toledo or Aranjuez?
A: Yes, the Tajo region offers routes accessible from various towns. For example, you can find challenging routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31844365" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of Toledo – View of the Palacio de Galiana loop from Toledo&lt;/a&gt; starting directly from Toledo. Near Aranjuez, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31494771" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Titulcia Main Square – Royal Palace of Aranjuez loop from Aranjuez&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of reservoirs or significant bridges?
A: Yes, several routes provide scenic views of the region's waterways and structures. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23743206" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Talavera de la Reina – Puente Azután Viewpoint loop from Talavera de la Reina&lt;/a&gt; offers views of the Azután Bridge. You can also find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4349035" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Pedro Bridge&lt;/a&gt; in the wider Tajo area.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the gravel trails?
A: The Alto Tajo Natural Park is known for its rich biodiversity. While specific sightings are never guaranteed, the extensive pine forests and well-preserved riverbanks provide habitats for various species. Keep an eye out for birds of prey, deer, and other local fauna, especially in the quieter, more remote sections of the park.
tips: Molina de Aragón, also known as the gateway between Castile and Aragon, is considered one of the most important points on the Camino del Cid and is a good base to explore the "Parque Natural del Alto Tajo". Even in his day, El Cid appreciated the hospitality shown to him by the Muslim ruler of Molina. He and his family enjoyed spending the night here on their journeys between Valencia and Castile. An overnight stay in Molina is just as worthwhile today as it was then, that's for sure.
Located on the Tagus River, next to the Taravilla lagoon, it is an idyllic place to take a bath from which you can contemplate views of the Tagus Valley.
There are several points where you can walk down to the river to cool off in its spectacular waters if it's hot.
In this place, the Tagus, barely born, forms a small waterfall. A place of great beauty and accessible by car if we want to start the route here

Hospedería Virgen de la Hoz is a charming rural accommodation. This place offers a peaceful and natural setting, ideal for relaxing and enjoying outdoor activities such as hiking, cycling and bird watching.
The inn has cosy and well-equipped rooms, a restaurant serving delicious à la carte dishes, and a terrace for relaxing. In addition, guests can enjoy Wi-Fi in public areas.
